commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
The PR exempts exact stdout/stderr-to-/dev/null and fd 1/2 duplication redirections from POSIX exec authorization planner blocking and adds behavioral tests for allowed and blocked redirect forms.

PR surface: Source +26, Tests +154. Total +180 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main treats every shell `redirect` risk as unanalyzable, and `requiresExecApproval` prompts on analysis misses in allowlist/on-miss mode.
commands.review_followup: - [P2] A narrow docs repair is needed; the runtime patch otherwise looks coherent, but it changes a documented exec approval boundary.

target.title: fix(exec): stop forcing approval for safe commands with harmless redirections
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

In `security: "allowlist"` mode, OpenClaw treats any command containing a shell redirection as an allowlist miss and forces a manual approval prompt — even when every command segment is already allowlisted/safe and the redirection only discards or merges a standard stream. Routine safe commands like `ls 2>/dev/null` or `grep … 2>&1 | head` interrupt the operator with an approval prompt despite touching no filesystem.

## Why This Change Was Made

Stream-only redirections — `>/dev/null`, `1>/dev/null`, `2>/dev/null`, `2>&1`, `1>&2` — are now classified as harmless and excluded from the unanalyzable-risk set in the POSIX authorization planner, so a command whose redirections are all harmless falls through to the existing per-segment allowlist/safeBins evaluation. Redirections never alter the parsed argv, so the same program-safety check still applies. Anything that writes or reads a real file (`> file`, `>> file`, `2> err.log`, `< in`), uses `&>`, closes/duplicates to an arbitrary fd (`>&-`, `2>&3`), or targets anything other than `/dev/null` or fd 1/2 still blocks. Non-goals: no change to which programs are considered safe, and no new config surface.

## User Impact

Operators running in allowlist mode no longer get approval prompts for safe commands that only redirect to `/dev/null` or merge stdout/stderr. The security posture is unchanged for any redirection that touches the filesystem — those still require approval exactly as before.

## Evidence

AI-assisted (Claude Code); reviewed with a Codex pass on both the plan and the diff.

New behavioral tests, green locally:

```
pnpm test src/infra/exec-authorization-plan.test.ts src/infra/exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts
  exec-authorization-plan.test.ts        61 passed
  exec-authorization-allowlist.test.ts    8 passed
```

Adjacent suites (no regressions) — exec-approvals-policy, exec-allow-always-persistence, exec-approvals-allow-always, exec-approvals-safe-bins, exec-approvals-parity, command-explainer/extract → **228 passed**.

Security evidence — disguised redirects that stay blocked (verified against the compiled `dist` build):

```
ls > out.txt                         → blocked
ls 2>error.log                       → blocked
cat < input.txt                      → blocked
ls >>/dev/null                       → blocked
ls &>/dev/null                       → blocked
head >/dev/null/../../../etc/crontab → blocked   (path traversal)
head 2>&3                            → blocked   (arbitrary fd)
head 3>/dev/null                     → blocked   (fd not 1/2)
head >| /dev/null                    → blocked   (noclobber override)
head >"/dev/null"                    → blocked   (quoted target)
$(whoami) 2>/dev/null                → blocked   (command-substitution unaffected)
```

End-to-end approval decision (compiled `dist`, real `head` as a default safe bin):

```
head -n 1 2>/dev/null         → no approval prompt
head -n 1 > /tmp/x            → approval prompt   (file write)
head -n 1 2>/dev/null; rm …   → approval prompt   (rm denied at allowlist layer)
```

Build `pnpm build` green; `pnpm tsgo` + `pnpm check:test-types` clean; `oxfmt` and `oxlint` clean.
target.author: bkf-gitty

commands.reason: repair commands require a ClawSweeper PR or a PR opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
